% The following was adapted from a definition generated by Gemini              %

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%

% Define colors for styling
\definecolor{neslkeywords}{rgb}{0,0,0.6}
\definecolor{neslfunctions}{rgb}{0.1,0.4,0.1}
\definecolor{nesltypes}{rgb}{0.5,0.2,0}
\definecolor{neslconstants}{rgb}{0.5,0,0.5}
\definecolor{neslstrings}{rgb}{0.7,0.1,0.1}
\definecolor{neslcomments}{rgb}{0.4,0.4,0.4}

% Define the NESL language for the listings package
\lstdefinelanguage{NESL}{
  % Treat these characters as keywords
  otherkeywords={|,\{,\},:},
  % Class 1: Primary keywords
  morekeywords={%
    if, then, else, let, in, function, datatype%
  },
  % Class 2: Built-in functions, word operators, and special operators
  keywords=[2]{%
    % Logical
    not, or, and, xor, nor, nand,%
    % Predicates
    plusp, minusp, zerop, oddp, evenp,%
    % Arithmetic
    abs, diff, max, min, rem, lshift, rshift, sqrt, isqrt, ln, log, exp, expt, sin, cos, tan, asin, acos, atan, sinh, cosh, tanh,%
    % Conversion
    btoi, code_char, char_code, float, ceil, floor, trunc, round,%
    % Other Scalar
    rand,%
    % Sequence Functions
    dist, elt, rep, zip, plus_scan, max_scan, min_scan, or_scan, and_scan, iseq, sum, max_val, min_val, any, all, count, max_index, min_index, permute, rotate, reverse, pack, subseq, drop, take, odd_elts, even_elts, interleave, partition, flatten, split, bottop, head_rest, rest_tail, sort, rank, collect, kth_smallest, search_for_subseqs, remove_duplicates, union, intersection, name,%
    % Misc Functions
    eql, hash, select,%
    % String Manipulation
    linify, wordify, lowercase, uppercase, string_eql, parse_int, parse_float,%
    % I/O and Side Effects
    print_char, print_string, print_debug, write_object_to_file, write_string_to_file, read_object_from_file, read_int_seq_from_file, read_float_seq_from_file, open_in_file, open_out_file, close_file, write_char, write_string, read_char, read_string, read_line, read_word, open_check, write_check, read_check, time,%
    % Plotting & Shell
    make_window, bounding_box, draw_points, draw_lines, draw_segments, draw_strings, get_mouse_info, close_window, shell_command, get_environment_variable, spawn%
  },
  % Class 3: Types and type classes
  keywords=[3]{%
    any, bool, char, float, int, logical, number, ordinal%
  },
  % Class 4: Constants
  keywords=[4]{t, f, pi, space, newline, tab},
  % Misc
  sensitive=false, % NESL is case-insensitive
  morestring=[b]", % Strings are enclosed in double quotes
  morestring=[b]'  % Characters are enclosed in single quotes
}

% Apply styling to the defined classes
\lstset{
  language=NESL,
  keywordstyle=\color{neslkeywords}\bfseries,
  keywordstyle=[2]\color{neslfunctions},
  keywordstyle=[3]\color{nesltypes},
  keywordstyle=[4]\color{neslconstants}\bfseries,
  stringstyle=\color{neslstrings},
  commentstyle=\color{neslcomments},
  basicstyle=\ttfamily\small,
  breaklines=true,
  breakatwhitespace=true,
  tabsize=2,
}

\begin{document}

\title{Village Synthesis}

\author{Liam Strand}
\affiliation{%
	\institution{Northwestern University}
	\city{Evanston}
	\state{IL}
	\country{USA}
}
\email{ltrs@u.northwestern.edu}
\orcid{0009-0005-9712-1689}

\begin{abstract}
	The cell divides and a hundred million years later, it's
	a bull shark or Sophocles. Not sure which; not sure
	how these things work. Point is:
	things change, evolve, move forward.
	You get the job, get promoted, get fired. Lead
	becomes gold, then one day it doesn't.
	The sun orbits the earth, then one day it doesn't.
	The whale gets legs, gets bored with land, goes back
	to the ocean, the legs are now a metaphor. Nothing certain,
	nothing written in stone that can't be unwritten by the
	hammer. The world keeps trying to end itself because it
	wants to end, then one day it doesn't.
\end{abstract}

\keywords{Heterogeneity, FPGA, HDL Synthesis, Nested-Data Parallelism}

\begin{teaserfigure}
	\begin{center}
		\digraph[width=0.95\textwidth]{teaser}{
		rankdir=LR;
		ranksep=1.2;
		node[shape=circle, fixedsize=true, width=1.0];
		{
		rank=same;
		"IN_I(0)";
		"IN_I(1)";
		"IN_B(2)";
		"IN_F(3)";
		}
		"IN_I(0)" -> ADD_I [headlabel="0 "];
		"IN_I(1)" -> ADD_I [headlabel="1 "];
		"IN_B(2)" -> NOT_B [headlabel="0 "];
		NOT_B -> B_TO_I [headlabel="0 "];
		ADD_I -> NOT_I [headlabel="0 "];
		NOT_I -> MUL_I [headlabel="0 "];
		B_TO_I -> MUL_I [headlabel="1 "];
		MUL_I -> I_TO_F [headlabel="0 "];
		I_TO_F -> DIV_F [headlabel="0 "];
		"IN_F(3)" -> DIV_F [headlabel="1 "];
		DIV_F -> F_TO_I [headlabel="0 "]
		F_TO_I -> "OUT_I(0)" [headlabel="0 "];
		}
		\caption{The abstract representation of a VIR operator dataflow subgraph that can now be lowered to hardware.}
		\label{fig:teaser}
	\end{center}
\end{teaserfigure}

\maketitle

\section{Introduction}

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%

\section{Background}

\subsection{Village}

Implementing complex algorithms in low-level parallel languages like CUDA \cite{NVIDIA:CUDA:Manual} or OpenMP \cite{dagum1998openmp} is challenging.
A direct implementation may be relatively easy, but taking full advantage of hardware capabilities often requires complex, inscrutable code that obfuscates the underlying algorithm.
High-level parallel languages, like Chapel \cite{chamberlain2015chapel}, Bend \cite{bend-hvm2}, or NESL \cite{BLELLOCH-NESL-JPDC94, BLELLOCH-NSEL-ICFP96} can ergonomically express parallel algorithms, but their implementations can struggle to be as performant as their low-level counterparts because the programmer does not have direct access to hardware resources.

The Village project \cite{village-hpdc25} provides a new implementation of NESL that aims to preserve it's expressive high-level parallel semantics and generate code that has the performance of a low-level parallel language.
The compiler generates a static executable by lowering NESL to VCODE (Section~\ref{sec:vcode}), then VCODE to VIR (Section~\ref{sec:vir}), VIR to LLVM-IR \cite{LATTNER:2004:LLVM}, and finally relying on LLVM's backend to produce machine code.
In this work, we intercept the compilation process at the VIR stage, and instead synthesize the program to a hardware design.

\subsubsection{NESL}

Designed in 1992 and implemented in 1996, NESL \cite{BLELLOCH-NESL-JPDC94, BLELLOCH-NSEL-ICFP96} is a high-level parallel language that uses the nested data parallelism model of computation.
This model allows programmers to express the parallelism of their algorithms in terms of applying a function in parallel to a set of values, where those values could themselves be parallel functions.
It is very flexible and allows the programmer to express all of the parallelism available in their algorithm.

\ltrs{this next sentence desperately needs to be rewritten.}
NESL is essentially just Standard ML with a sprinkling of parallelism.
There is a parallel map/filter operator and collection types for that operator to consume.
Recursion semantics are also updated to allow for parallel recursive calls.

\begin{figure}
	\begin{center}
		\begin{lstlisting}[language=NESL, numbers=left]
function qsort(a) =
  if (#a < 2) then a
  else let 
  	pivot   = a[#a/2];
    lesser  = {e in a | e < pivot};
    equal   = {e in a | e == pivot};
    greater = {e in a | e > pivot};
    result  = {qsort(v) : v in [lesser, greater]}
  in result[0] ++ equal ++ result[1];
		\end{lstlisting}
	\end{center}
	\caption{A NESL implementation of quicksort, exposing the parallelism of the scan over the collection \texttt{a} and the parallel recursive calls to \texttt{qsort}.}\label{fig:nesl-sample}
\end{figure}

Figure~\ref{fig:nesl-sample} contains the NESL implementation of quicksort, a classic parallel algorithm.
Lines 5-7 express flat data parallelism, with each line applying a filter to the collection.
Line 8 shows the recursive calls over the two subcollections, which can be executed in parallel.
A flat data parallel language would struggle to reveal the parallelism available in quicksort so succinctly and clearly.

\subsubsection{VCODE}
\label{sec:vcode}

\ltrs{need to do a language pass on this section}
Since NESL would be far too complex to execute directly at any speed, it is first lowered to VCODE.
VCODE uses a (relatively) small set of instructions that operate on a stack of segmented vectors, further simplifying an implementation.
This translation process maps nested parallelism to equivalent flat parallelism.
This means that parallel recursive calls to parts of a collection, as in line 8 of Figure~\ref{fig:nesl-sample}, are reduced to a single call over a segmented vector.
Although this flattening simplifies the implementation of a VCODE interpreter, it is still able to represent nearly all of the parallelism available in the original NESL program.

Translating a stack-based intermediate representation such as VCODE into a hardware design would be a monumental task.
Fortunately, prior work allows VCODE to be translated to another intermediate representation that is much more amenable to lowering to hardware.

\subsubsection{VIR}
\label{sec:vir}

A good intermediate representation for hardware synthesis would feature both relatively simple operations and a data\-flow representation of the connections between operations.
VIR is a vector dataflow intermediate representation that uses instructions nearly identical to those of VCODE.
Instead of a stack of vectors with global state, VIR is just a dataflow graph connecting inputs to outputs.
The textual syntax of VIR is represented as a sequential single-static-assignment program, but the backing representation is of a true dependency graph between operators.

\begin{figure}
	\begin{center}
		\begin{lstlisting}[numbers=left]
(%V1) = vcode_not_bool(%A2)
(%V2) = vcode_add_int(%A0, %A1)
(%V3) = vcode_bool_to_int(%V1)
(%V4) = vcode_not_int(%V2)
(%V5) = vcode_mul_int(%V3, %V4)
(%V6) = vcode_int_to_float(%V5)
(%V7) = vcode_div_float(%V6, %A3)
(%V8) = vcode_float_to_int(%V7)
		\end{lstlisting}
	\end{center}
	\caption{A simple VIR program that includes all three datatypes, and unary and binary operators.}
	\label{fig:vir-sample}
\end{figure}

A dataflow graph representation is very helpful for hardware synthesis because it makes the dependencies between operations explicit.
In hardware design, computations are performed by physical functional units that are wired together.
A dataflow graph serves as a direct blueprint for these connections, mapping how the output of one operation feeds into the input of another.
This explicit representation of data movement is much easier to translate into a physical circuit than a stack-based representation like VCODE, where dependencies are implicit in the order of push and pop operations on a shared stack.

Figure~\ref{fig:vir-sample} contains many operations that are interdependent.
We see, for example, that \texttt{vcode\_div\_float} on line 7 must wait for \texttt{vcode\_int\_to\_float} on line 6, and so on back to \texttt{vcode\_not\_bool} and \texttt{vcode\_add\_int} on lines 1 and 2, which have no dependencies.
For a graphical representation of these dependencies, see Figure~\ref{fig:teaser}.

VIR is a representation that is significantly closer to the physical structure of a hardware circuit, which greatly simplifies the task of synthesizing the high-level algorithm into hardware.

\subsection{Chisel}

To perform the synthesis, we rely on Chisel \cite{chisel}, a new hardware description language embedded in Scala.
Chisel code takes the form of \emph{generators}, which are Scala classes that can be invoked by the Chisel compiler to generate a specific instance of a hardware module.
Generators can take parameters and, since this is Scala, they are highly composable using both object-oriented and functional programming paradigms.

During compilation, the Scala code is executed to generate the circuit in an intermediate representation called FIRRTL (Flexible Intermediate Representation for RTL).
This FIRRTL representation is then passed to a separate compiler which performs hardware-specific optimizations before emitting a final, low-level hardware description such as Verilog.

Chisel has been used to build many complex hardware projects, including high-performance RISC-V cores like the in-order Rocket \cite{rocket-core} and the out-of-order SonicBOOM \cite{boom-core}.
Moreover, Chisel is the foundation of the Chipyard \cite{chipyard} framework for SoC design, simulation, and synthesis.

In this work, Chisel is used to map VIR nodes to hardware functional units, wire up the connections between those functional units, and wrap the graph of functional units in an interface that can be accessed as a discrete accelerator from the outside world.

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%

\section{Operator Graphs in Scala}

In order to transform a VIR graph into a hardware design, we must first bring the VIR graph into the Chisel ecosystem.

\begin{figure}
	\begin{center}
		\begin{lstlisting}[language=scala]
sealed trait VillageOperand
case object BOOL extends VillageOperand
case object INT extends VillageOperand
case object FLOAT extends VillageOperand

sealed trait VcodeNode {
		val inputs:  Seq[VillageOperand]
		val outputs: Seq[VillageOperand]
		val id: Int = VcodeNode.next()
}
		\end{lstlisting}
	\end{center}
	\caption{The \texttt{VcodeNode} type that is used to internally represent a VIR graph within Scala.}
	\label{fig:village-graph-node-def}
\end{figure}

\subsection{Node Contents}

Each VIR node is represented as a subclass of a \texttt{VcodeNode}, which provides them with a globally unique identifier and requires that the node specifies its I/O (see Figure~\ref{fig:village-graph-node-def}).
An addition, for example, takes two integer inputs and produces one integer output.
This interface described to Scala as in Figure~\ref{fig:village-graph-addi-def}.

\begin{figure}
	\begin{center}
		\begin{lstlisting}[language=scala]
sealed case class ADD_I() extends VcodeNode {
		val inputs = Seq(INT, INT); 
		val outputs = Seq(INT) 
}
		\end{lstlisting}
	\end{center}
	\caption{A simple binary operator node class.}
	\label{fig:village-graph-addi-def}
\end{figure}

To interact with the outside world, the graph must define where inputs are supplied and outputs are read.
I/O nodes serve this purpose.
From the perspective of the \texttt{VcodeGraph}, an input node produces a single output of the specified type and an output node consumes a single input of the specified type.
Each I/O node is also assigned an index number to specify the order in which the inputs and outputs appear when combined in the accelerator's I/O.

This complex object-oriented-class-hierarchy-mixin preamble is necessary to allow future generation steps to pattern match on the type of node to dispatch to the correct hardware generator.

\subsection{\texttt{scala-graph} DSL}

It is not enough to have nodes alone, we must also represent the edges between nodes!
To accomplish this we take advantage of the full-featured \texttt{scala-graph} \cite{scala-graph} library.
\texttt{scala-graph} allows us to create a typed graph of \texttt{VcodeNode}s, that are linked by labeled, directed edges.
We need the edges to be labeled because some VIR operations are not commutative, meaning that the ordering of their inputs matters.
To encode this ordering, each edge is labeled with which input it should be attached to on the target node.

These graphs can be imported as JSON or DOT, and they are endlessly queryable and transformable from within Scala.

\begin{figure}
	\begin{center}
		\begin{lstlisting}[language=scala]
val add = new ADD_I
val graph = VcodeGraph.from(Seq(
		new INPUT_I(0) ~> add :+ 0,
		new INPUT_I(1) ~> add :+ 1,
		add ~> new OUTPUT_B(0) :+ 0
))
		\end{lstlisting}
	\end{center}
	\caption{A small \texttt{VcodeGraph} that adds two numbers together using the \texttt{scala-graph} DSL.}
	\label{fig:village-graph-def}
\end{figure}

As a bonus, \texttt{scala-graph} also uses Scala implicits to create a domain-specific language for describing these VIR graphs.
Figure~\ref{fig:village-graph-def} shows an example of this DSL in-use.
The \texttt{\textasciitilde>} operator denotes a directed edge and the \texttt{:+} operator assigns the edge label.
The DSL is very helpful when defining graphs for unit testing, or for quick compilation and synthesis checks.

\subsection{Graph Validation}

This representation permits any layout of nodes, with any set of edges connecting them.
Many, many graphs can be represented that cannot be synthesized to hardware.
Some examples include cycles, disconnected graphs, and graphs with duplicate edge labels.
Before we can begin the synthesis process, we must validate that the graph is in fact synthesizable.
The properties asserted are as follows:

\BEGINITEMIZE
\item The graph is acyclic.
\item The graph is connected.
\item A node's incoming edge count matches its logical input count.
\item A node's outgoing edge count matches its logical output count.
\item Each node's incoming edges are numbered sequentially from 0.
\item The input and output ends of each edge are of the same type.
\item The inputs and output nodes are numbered sequentially from 0.
\ENDITEMIZE

Two properties follow from these assertions.
First, the set of input nodes and the set of output nodes can each be joined into a bus. \ltrs{``bus'' is the wrong word}
Second, every node is connected to the correct number of inputs and outputs that will supply and consume values of the correct type.


%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%

\section{Hardware Functional Unit Library}

Each node in the \texttt{VcodeGraph} needs to be mapped to a hardware module.
Many of these modules are quite simple, needing only a few lines of Chisel code.
Others, specifically floating point operations, are much more complex.

\begin{figure}
	\begin{center}
		\begin{lstlisting}[language=scala]
trait HasVillageIO {
  	val io: Bundle {
  			val in: Vec[DecoupledIO[UInt]];
  			val out: DecoupledIO[UInt];
  	}
}
abstract class VillageFunctionalUnit(n: VcodeNode) 
		extends Module with HasVillageIO
		{ /* bookkeeping */ }
		\end{lstlisting}
	\end{center}
	\caption{The interface shared between all functional units.}
	\label{fig:fu-interface}
\end{figure}

This complexity is hidden behind a shared interface that is implemented by all functional units.
The interface, shown in Figure~\ref{fig:fu-interface}, specifies that all inputs and outputs are \emph{decoupled}, meaning that the producer must mark the data as \texttt{valid} and the consumer must be \texttt{ready} to receive it before the transfer takes place.
Moreover, it places all inputs in a single \texttt{Vec} that can be indexed into, and limits each functional unit to a single output.
Removing this limitation is a work-in-progress.

Each functional unit extends the \texttt{VillageFunctionalUnit} class and uses I/O provided by one of the \texttt{HasVillageIO} specializations (see Section~\ref{sec:hasvillageio-specializations}).

\subsection{Simple Unary and Binary Functional Units}

Most VIR instructions are simple unary and binary arithmetic operations.
\ltrs{I need to find a new sentence structure. The ..., meaning ... is getting tiresome.}
These functional units are small enough that they can execute in a single cycle, meaning that once their operands become \texttt{valid}, they can produce a \texttt{valid} result in the same cycle.

This simplification allows the Scala generator to be parameterized over the operation that needs to be performed.
The same generator can be used for an integer addition, bitwise XOR, or boolean equality.
In lines 2 and 3 of Figure~\ref{fig:fu-instantiation}, we see the generators used to create a \texttt{NOT\_I} and a \texttt{ADD\_I} functional unit.
Of the 42 functional units defined in this work, 21 use either the \texttt{VillageBinaryFU} generator or the \texttt{VillageUnaryFU} generator.

Since internal I/O is delegated to \texttt{HasVillageIO} specializations, the actual implementation of each of these generators requires only six lines of code. Moreover, the implementation of a functional unit using one of these generators requires just a single line of code.

\begin{figure}
	\begin{center}
		\begin{lstlisting}[language=scala,numbers=left]
val functional_units = Seq(
	new VillageUnaryFU(new NOT_I(),  ~_),
  new VillageBinaryFU(new ADD_I(), _+_),
	new VillageIO(new INPUT_I(0)),
	new VillageFloatAddFU(new ADD_F()),
	new VillageFloatCmpFU(new EQ_F(), EQ),
	new VillageFloatToIntFU(new TRUNC_F(), TRUNC)
)
		\end{lstlisting}
	\end{center}
	\caption{Instantiating several different functional units.}
	\label{fig:fu-instantiation}
\end{figure}

\subsection{Input and Output}

Input functional units receive their input from the outside world and provide output to functional units that perform computations.
Since all connections between functional units are 64 bits wide (another restriction whose removal is a work-in-progress), boolean inputs are first or-reduced so that the integer value of the wire is either 0 or 1.

On the output side, functional units receive their input from some other functional unit and provide output to the outside world.
Output functional units also or-reduce boolean values before emitting them.

The outside world sees these nodes through the accelerator interface, which is described in Section~\ref{sec:accelerator-io}.

\subsection{Floating Point with \texttt{hardfloat}}

Floating point functional units are by far the most complicated in this system.
While floating point addition can be completed in a single cycle, division and square root can be painfully slow.
Moreover, implementing a high-performance floating point computation in hardware is quite difficult.
Fortunately, the \texttt{hardfloat} library \cite{berkeley-hardfloat} provides high-quality implementations of all basic floating point operations.

\texttt{hardfloat} uses a recoded version of the IEEE 754 standard, so each floating point functional unit first converts its inputs from IEEE 754 format to the recoded format before passing the bits off to the appropriate \texttt{hardfloat} module.
When ready to emit a result, the output is then reformatted back to adhere to the IEEE 754 standard.
This way, all floating point values in wires or in visible registers are in IEEE 754 format, rather than having some recoded and some not.
Since the reformatting operation is a simple matter of bit swizzling, the overhead is negligible.

There are additional challenges to consider when designing functional units for multicycle computations like division or square root.
The \texttt{hardfloat} modules signal when they are \texttt{ready} for input and when their outputs are \texttt{valid}.
These signals need to be wired up to the functional unit I/O so that the \texttt{hardfloat} module does not get swamped with operands.
The \texttt{hardfloat} division module specifically does support some overlapping of operands, but we are unfortunately unable to take advantage of this feature at this time.

Floating point comparisons are also an interesting challenge because \texttt{hardfloat} provides one ``total-comparison'' module rather than a separate module for each type of comparison (greater-than, equal, etc).
Luckily, Chisel can come to the rescue.
The floating point comparison generator takes a parameter that selects the comparison to perform.
In line 6 of Figure~\ref{fig:fu-instantiation}, the generator is passed the \texttt{EQ} flag to emit a functional unit that checks for floating point equality.

\subsection{Conversions}

Finally, we arrive at the last category of functional unit: type conversions.
We may want to convert an integer into a boolean, or round a floating point value into an integer.
Converting between integers and booleans is straightforward.
To transform a boolean into an integer we simply emit 1 if the input is nonzero and 0 otherwise.
Casting an integer to a boolean is identical.

Conversions to and from floating point values are, as usual, more complex.
\texttt{hardfloat} provides modules to perform these conversions, which both accept as input a ``rounding mode'' signal.
For conversions from integers to floats, the module is always configured to round to the nearest representable number.
There are three separate VIR instructions for rounding from a float to an integer: ceiling, floor, and truncation (round towards zero).
Each of these instructions is mapped to a functional unit that is preconfigured to perform the appropriate rounding operation. An instantiation of the functional unit for \texttt{TRUNC\_F} is shown in line 7 of Figure~\ref{fig:fu-instantiation}.
